description: A cell where only one digit is possible given the constraints of its row, column, and 3x3 box. All other eight digits already appear somewhere in the same row, column, or box.
when_to_apply: Scan every empty cell and check how many candidates remain. When only one candidate is left in a cell, place that digit immediately. This is the most fundamental technique and should always be checked first before any other strategy.
example: If a cell's row contains 1-8 and its column or box supplies the 9th digit, only one value can go in that cell.
eliminates: N/A — directly places a digit

description: A digit that can only be placed in one cell within a given unit (row, column, or 3x3 box), even though that cell may appear to have multiple candidates. The correct digit is 'hidden' among other candidates in the cell.
when_to_apply: For each unit (row, column, box), scan each digit 1-9 and identify how many cells could hold it. When only one cell in the unit can contain a particular digit, place it there regardless of how many other candidates that cell has.
example: In a row where the digit 7 can only fit in one cell (because 7 already appears in the same column or box for every other empty cell in that row), place 7 in that cell.
eliminates: N/A — directly places a digit

description: Two cells in the same unit (row, column, or box) that together contain exactly two candidate digits and no others. Because those two digits must occupy exactly those two cells, they can be eliminated from all other cells in the shared unit.
when_to_apply: Look for two cells in the same unit that share exactly the same two candidates (e.g., both contain only [3,7]). Once found, remove those two digits from every other cell in that unit.
example: Cells A2 and A3 both contain only candidates [1,6]. Therefore 1 and 6 can be removed from all other cells in row A.
eliminates: The two paired digits from all other cells in the shared unit

description: Three cells in the same unit that collectively contain exactly three candidate digits (each cell may have two or three of those digits). The three digits are locked into those three cells and can be eliminated from all other cells in the unit.
when_to_apply: Look for any three cells in the same unit whose combined candidate set contains exactly three digits. The cells do not each need all three digits — combinations like [1,2], [2,3], [1,3] or [1,2,3], [1,2], [2,3] all qualify. Remove those three digits from the rest of the unit.
example: Cells E4, E5, E6 contain [5,8,9], [5,8], and [5,9] respectively. Together they hold only {5,8,9}, so 5, 8, and 9 can be removed from all other cells in row E.
eliminates: The three grouped digits from all other cells in the shared unit

description: Four cells in the same unit that collectively contain exactly four candidate digits. Those four digits are locked into those four cells and can be eliminated from all other cells in the unit. Rare in practice because a complementary Naked or Hidden Triple is usually found first.
when_to_apply: Look for four cells in the same unit whose combined candidates form a set of exactly four digits. Remove those four digits from every other cell in the unit.
example: Cells A1, B1, B2, C1 in box 1 collectively contain only [1,5,6,8]. Those four digits can be removed from all other cells in box 1.
eliminates: The four grouped digits from all other cells in the shared unit

description: Two digits that can only appear in exactly two cells within a unit. Even though those cells may have other candidates, the two digits must go in those two cells, so all other candidates in those two cells can be eliminated.
when_to_apply: For each unit, scan pairs of digits and find any two digits that are confined to exactly the same two cells. When found, remove all other candidates from those two cells.
example: In a column, digits 4 and 7 can only go in cells C3 and C8. Remove all other candidates from C3 and C8, leaving only [4,7] in each.
eliminates: All other candidates from the two cells that host the hidden pair

description: Three digits that are collectively confined to exactly three cells within a unit. All other candidates in those three cells can be eliminated.
when_to_apply: Scan each unit for any three digits that appear only in a set of exactly three cells. Remove all other candidates from those three cells.
example: In a box, digits 2, 5, and 9 only appear in cells R1C1, R1C3, and R3C2. Remove all other candidates from those three cells.
eliminates: All other candidates from the three cells that host the hidden triple

description: Four digits confined to exactly four cells within a unit. All other candidates in those four cells can be eliminated. Extremely rare in practice.
when_to_apply: Scan each unit for any four digits that appear only in a set of exactly four cells. Remove all other candidates from those four cells.
example: In a row, digits 1, 3, 6, and 9 only appear in four specific cells. Remove all other candidates from those four cells.
eliminates: All other candidates from the four cells that host the hidden quad

description: When a candidate digit within a 3x3 box is restricted to a single row or column, that digit can be eliminated from the rest of that row or column outside the box. The digit is 'pointing' along the row or column.
when_to_apply: For each box, examine each candidate digit. If all occurrences of that digit within the box lie in the same row or column, eliminate that digit from all other cells in that row or column outside the box.
example: In box 1, the digit 5 only appears in row A (cells A1 and A2). Therefore 5 can be removed from all other cells in row A outside box 1.
eliminates: The pointing digit from the rest of the row or column outside the box

description: When a candidate digit within a row or column is confined to a single 3x3 box, that digit can be eliminated from all other cells in that box. The row or column is 'claiming' the digit for that box.
when_to_apply: For each row and column, examine each candidate digit. If all occurrences of that digit in the row/column lie within the same box, eliminate that digit from all other cells in that box.
example: In row D, the digit 3 only appears in cells that belong to box 5. Therefore 3 can be removed from all other cells in box 5 that are not in row D.
eliminates: The claimed digit from the rest of the box

description: A pattern involving one candidate digit that appears in exactly two cells in each of two different rows (or columns), and those cells share the same two columns (or rows). The digit can be eliminated from all other cells in those two columns (or rows).
when_to_apply: For a given digit, find two rows where the digit appears as a candidate in exactly two cells, and those cells are in the same two columns. The digit can then be eliminated from all other cells in those two columns. The same logic applies with rows and columns swapped.
example: Digit 6 appears as a candidate in only two cells in row B (columns 3 and 7) and only two cells in row H (also columns 3 and 7). This X-Wing allows 6 to be eliminated from all other cells in columns 3 and 7.
eliminates: The X-Wing digit from all other cells in the two shared columns (or rows)

description: An extension of X-Wing to three rows and three columns. A candidate digit appears in exactly two or three cells in each of three rows, and all those cells are confined to the same three columns. The digit can be eliminated from all other cells in those three columns.
when_to_apply: For a given digit, find three rows where the digit appears in only two or three cells each, and all those cells fall within the same three columns. Eliminate the digit from all other cells in those three columns. The same logic applies with rows and columns swapped.
example: Digit 4 appears in at most three cells in rows A, E, and H, and all occurrences fall in columns 2, 5, and 9. Remove 4 from all other cells in columns 2, 5, and 9.
eliminates: The Swordfish digit from all other cells in the three shared columns (or rows)

description: A technique that uses the fact that a candidate digit in a unit with exactly two possible cells must be in one cell or the other. By alternately colouring these 'conjugate pairs' across the board, cells that see two cells of the same colour can have the digit eliminated.
when_to_apply: For a given digit, find all units where it appears in exactly two cells (conjugate pairs). Build a chain by alternately colouring connected cells. If two cells of the same colour can see each other, that colour is wrong and all cells of that colour can have the digit removed. If a cell outside the chain can see cells of both colours, the digit can be removed from that cell.
example: Digit 7 forms a chain of conjugate pairs. Two 'green' cells in the chain both appear in the same row, so green is the wrong colour — remove 7 from all green cells.
eliminates: The digit from cells that see two same-coloured cells, or all cells of the contradicting colour

description: A three-cell pattern where a 'pivot' cell contains exactly two candidates [A,B], and two 'pincer' cells each share one candidate with the pivot ([A,C] and [B,C]). Any cell that can see both pincers cannot contain candidate C.
when_to_apply: Find a bi-value cell (pivot) with candidates [A,B]. Look for two other bi-value cells that can each see the pivot — one with [A,C] and one with [B,C]. Any cell visible to both pincers can have C eliminated, because whichever value the pivot takes, one of the pincers must be C.
example: Pivot cell has [3,7]. Pincer 1 has [3,5] and can see the pivot. Pincer 2 has [7,5] and can see the pivot. Any cell that sees both pincers cannot be 5.
eliminates: Candidate C from all cells that can see both pincer cells

description: An extension of Y-Wing where the pivot cell has three candidates [X,Y,Z] instead of two. The two pincers have candidates [X,Z] and [Y,Z]. Any cell that can see the pivot and both pincers cannot contain Z.
when_to_apply: Find a tri-value cell (pivot) with candidates [X,Y,Z]. Look for two bi-value cells that can each see the pivot — one with [X,Z] and one with [Y,Z]. Any cell that can see all three cells (pivot and both pincers) can have Z eliminated.
example: Pivot has [1,5,9]. Pincer 1 has [1,9] and pincer 2 has [5,9]. Any cell seeing all three cannot be 9.
eliminates: Candidate Z from cells that can see the pivot and both pincers

description: A chain of bi-value cells where each consecutive pair shares one candidate. The chain starts and ends with the same candidate digit. Any cell that can see both ends of the chain cannot contain that digit.
when_to_apply: Build a chain of bi-value cells where each cell shares one candidate with the next. If the first and last candidate in the chain are the same digit, any cell that can see both the start and end of the chain can have that digit eliminated. If the chain forms a closed loop, additional eliminations are possible along every link.
example: Chain: [5,9]→[9,2]→[2,6]→[6,5]. The chain starts and ends with 5. Any cell seeing both endpoints cannot be 5.
eliminates: The shared end-candidate from all cells that can see both chain endpoints

description: A technique based on the assumption that a valid Sudoku has exactly one solution. If two digits appear as candidates in exactly four cells forming a rectangle that spans exactly two boxes, allowing both digits to be interchangeable would create two solutions. This impossibility allows candidate eliminations.
when_to_apply: Find four cells forming a rectangle (two rows, two columns) that span exactly two boxes, where all four cells contain at least two common candidates. Apply uniqueness logic: if placing the two common candidates in both possible orientations would both be valid, the puzzle would have two solutions — which is impossible. Use this contradiction to eliminate candidates.
example: Cells A1, A5, E1, E5 all contain candidates [2,8] among others. If 2 and 8 were the only candidates in all four cells, there would be two solutions. Therefore, at least one cell must have an additional candidate that forces a unique resolution.
eliminates: Candidates that would create a deadly pattern (non-unique rectangle)

description: An extension of Swordfish to four rows and four columns. A candidate digit appears in exactly two, three, or four cells in each of four rows, and all those cells are confined to the same four columns. The digit can be eliminated from all other cells in those four columns.
when_to_apply: For a given digit, find four rows where the digit appears in only two to four cells each, and all occurrences fall within the same four columns. Eliminate the digit from all other cells in those four columns. The same logic applies with rows and columns swapped. Jellyfish is rare and usually only needed in very hard puzzles.
example: Digit 3 appears in at most four cells in rows B, D, F, and H, all confined to columns 1, 4, 6, and 9. Remove 3 from all other cells in those four columns.
eliminates: The Jellyfish digit from all other cells in the four shared columns (or rows)

description: A technique that follows the logical consequences of assuming a candidate is true or false. If assuming a candidate leads to a contradiction, that candidate can be eliminated. If both assumptions lead to the same result for another cell, that result must be true.
when_to_apply: When all simpler techniques are exhausted. Assume a candidate in a cell is true and follow the chain of forced consequences. If a contradiction arises (e.g., a unit has no valid cell for a digit), the assumed candidate is false and can be eliminated. If both possible values for a cell lead to the same digit being placed in another cell, that placement is certain.
example: Assume digit 4 in cell A1. Following the chain of forced placements leads to cell B3 having no valid candidates — a contradiction. Therefore 4 cannot be in A1.
eliminates: Candidates that lead to contradictions, or confirms placements forced by all branches

description: A set of N cells in a unit that collectively contain exactly N+1 candidates. Because one extra candidate exists beyond what is needed to fill the cells, the extra candidate is 'almost locked'. ALS structures can be chained to make powerful eliminations.
when_to_apply: Identify groups of N cells in a unit with exactly N+1 candidates. When two ALS groups share a 'restricted common' candidate (a digit that can only go in one ALS or the other), the other candidates that appear in both ALS groups can be eliminated from cells that see all instances of those candidates in both groups.
example: ALS-A: cells [R1C1, R1C2] with candidates [1,3,5]. ALS-B: cells [R4C1, R4C3] with candidates [1,4,5]. Digit 1 is the restricted common. Digit 5 appears in both ALS groups, so 5 can be eliminated from cells that see all 5s in both groups.
eliminates: Shared non-restricted candidates visible to all instances in both ALS groups

description: An extension of Simple Colouring that works across multiple digits simultaneously. Cells with exactly two candidates are coloured, and the colouring propagates through conjugate pairs of any digit. Contradictions and eliminations are found when cells see two same-coloured candidates.
when_to_apply: Build a colouring network starting from any bi-value cell or conjugate pair. Propagate colours through all connected bi-value cells and conjugate pairs across all digits. Apply four elimination rules: (1) if two same-coloured candidates appear in the same cell, that colour is wrong; (2) if two same-coloured candidates appear in the same unit, that colour is wrong; (3) uncoloured candidates in a cell where all other candidates are coloured can be eliminated; (4) uncoloured candidates that see both colours of the same digit can be eliminated.
example: A colouring network assigns 'orange' and 'blue' to candidates across the board. Two orange 7s appear in the same column — contradiction. All orange candidates are eliminated.
eliminates: Candidates based on colouring contradictions and visibility rules
